title: WWE | History, Champions, Shows, Events 2026
subtitle: The global leader in sports entertainment, operating Raw, SmackDown, NXT, and the annual WrestleMania franchise since 1963
content_html:
Overview | What Is WWE

World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE) is the largest professional wrestling organization in the world, blending athletic competition with scripted storytelling across weekly television, streaming, and live events. As of 2026, WWE operates three flagship brands: Raw on Netflix, SmackDown on USA Network, and NXT, its developmental and stand-alone brand. The company produces more than 500 hours of original content annually and runs live events in over 60 countries.

Since January 2025, Raw moved to Netflix as part of a landmark 10-year streaming deal valued at approximately $5 billion, marking the first time a major American live sports and entertainment program migrated exclusively to a streaming platform. The move brought WWE to an estimated 300 million Netflix households worldwide.

WWE is now a subsidiary of TKO Group Holdings, the publicly traded company formed in September 2023 through the merger of WWE and UFC parent Endeavor. For more on the corporate structure, see the TKO Era section below.

WWE History | 1952 to 2026

WWE traces its origins to the Capitol Wrestling Corporation, founded in 1952 by Jess McMahon and Toots Mondt. In 1963, the organization affiliated with the National Wrestling Alliance before breaking away to operate independently as the World Wide Wrestling Federation (WWWF). The McMahon family has controlled the company across multiple generations, with Vince McMahon taking majority ownership in 1982.

The National Expansion | 1982 to 1993

When Vince McMahon purchased the company from his father in 1982, the wrestling industry was largely regional. McMahon aggressively signed talent from competing territories and launched a national television strategy, poaching stars like Hulk Hogan, Roddy Piper, and Andre the Giant. The first WrestleMania in March 1985 at Madison Square Garden, featuring celebrity involvement from Mr. T and Cyndi Lauper, demonstrated that professional wrestling could cross into mainstream entertainment.

The late 1980s saw WWE's first documented pay-per-view boom, with events like SummerSlam (1988) and Survivor Series (1987) establishing a pay-per-view calendar that persists to this day. The era was defined by the Hulkamania period, with Hulk Hogan as the company's signature box office draw from 1984 to 1992.

The Monday Night Wars | 1995 to 2001

The most competitive period in wrestling history began in September 1995 when Ted Turner's World Championship Wrestling (WCW) launched Monday Nitro on TNT, directly opposing WWF Raw on USA Network. WCW recruited major WWF stars including Scott Hall, Kevin Nash, and, most significantly, Hulk Hogan, who turned heel to lead the New World Order (nWo) faction in July 1996.

WCW dominated the ratings war for 83 consecutive weeks from mid-1996 to early 1998. WWF's turnaround came via the Attitude Era, characterized by edgier content, adult storylines, and the rise of Steve Austin and The Rock. WWF reclaimed the ratings lead in 1998, and by 2001 purchased WCW outright for $4.2 million when AOL Time Warner cancelled all wrestling programming.

The Ruthless Aggression and PG Eras | 2002 to 2013

Following the WCW acquisition, WWE split its roster into two distinct brands: Raw and SmackDown. This Brand Extension created separate title lineages and talent rosters, with stars like Brock Lesnar, John Cena, Batista, and Randy Orton emerging as the company's top draws. The company transitioned to a PG content rating in 2008, coinciding with its push into emerging markets and international broadcast deals.

The Reality Era and NXT Rise | 2013 to 2019

Triple H's creative influence expanded significantly from 2012 onward, particularly through NXT, the developmental brand he rebuilt into a full standalone show. NXT became critically acclaimed as the promotion that developed talent including Seth Rollins, Dean Ambrose (Jon Moxley), and Roman Reigns as The Shield, as well as international signings like Finn Balor, Shinsuke Nakamura, and Asuka.

The launch of the WWE Network in February 2014 at $9.99/month disrupted the pay-per-view business model and reached over 1.5 million subscribers within its first year.

The Peacock Deal and Saudi Partnership | 2019 to 2023

In 2019, WWE signed a multi-year partnership with Saudi Arabia for premium live events, beginning with the Greatest Royal Rumble. The partnership, reported at over $450 million over five years, generated significant controversy over Saudi Arabia's human rights record. Domestically, WWE Network content migrated to NBCUniversal's Peacock streaming platform in 2021 as part of a deal worth approximately $1 billion over five years.

TKO Era | WWE Under Endeavor, 2023 to Present

In April 2023, WWE announced it would merge with UFC under Endeavor Group Holdings to form a new publicly traded entity. TKO Group Holdings began trading on the New York Stock Exchange on September 12, 2023, under the ticker symbol TKO. Endeavor CEO Ariel Emanuel became CEO of TKO, with WWE founder Vince McMahon initially serving as executive chairman before his resignation in January 2024 amid a federal investigation.

The TKO era fundamentally changed WWE's business strategy. Triple H (Paul Levesque), who had been promoting NXT for over a decade, was elevated to Chief Content Officer, taking creative control of Raw and SmackDown. His tenure is characterized by long-term storytelling, returns of established acts, and a premium-event reduction from over a dozen annual shows to six major tentpole events.

The Raw on Netflix deal, announced in January 2024 and launched in January 2025, is the centerpiece of the TKO strategy. The 10-year, $5 billion deal with Netflix represented the largest media rights agreement in WWE history, eclipsing previous US deals with USA Network and Fox.

In April 2026, WWE confirmed that Triple H signed a new multi-year deal as Chief Content Officer, cementing his role through at least 2029. His creative approach continues to focus on the Bloodline storyline and a rebuilt pay-per-view premium live event calendar. WWE stockholders have also reportedly filed a lawsuit over the original WWE-Endeavor sale terms, according to reporting from Puck News via PWInsider .

Current WWE Shows | Raw, SmackDown, NXT

Raw | Monday Nights on Netflix

Monday Night Raw debuted on January 11, 1993 on USA Network and has run continuously for over 33 years, making it one of the longest-running episodic TV programs in American history. In January 2025, Raw moved exclusively to Netflix worldwide, airing live on Monday nights at 8/7c. The show runs three hours and serves as the primary platform for the World Heavyweight Championship and Women's World Championship storylines.

As of April 2026, Raw is headlined by the Roman Reigns vs. Jacob Fatu feud building toward Backlash. Other major Raw storylines include Seth Rollins vs. Bron Breakker, IYO SKY vs. Asuka vs. Becky Lynch, and Oba Femi's open challenge run.

SmackDown | Friday Nights on USA Network

SmackDown premiered in August 1999 and moved to Fox for a five-year deal (2019-2024) before returning to USA Network in 2025. The show airs on Friday nights and is home to the Undisputed WWE Championship and Women's Championship. The April 24, 2026 episode saw Jacob Fatu defeat Solo Sikoa before destroying The MFTs, furthering the Bloodline civil war heading into Backlash.

NXT | Tuesday Nights on USA Network

NXT evolved from a developmental show to WWE's third brand in 2019. The brand runs its own championship structure including the NXT Championship, North American Championship, and Women's North American Championship. NXT Revenge 2026 aired in April, with Zaria defeating Sol Ruca in a Last Woman Standing match to close out the two-week special event.

Current WWE Champions | April 2026

Roman Reigns captured the World Heavyweight Championship at WrestleMania 42 by defeating CM Punk. The Bloodline civil war, which pits Reigns against former stablemate Jacob Fatu, is WWE's current marquee storyline. Fatu, who had been aligned with Solo Sikoa's Bloodline faction before turning, has emerged as one of the promotion's most physically dominant characters.

WWE Backlash 2026 | Confirmed Card

WWE Backlash 2026 is the next major premium live event following WrestleMania 42. As of April 28, 2026, three matches have been officially announced. The event is expected to take place in May 2026.

On the April 27 episode of Raw, Jacob Fatu attacked Roman Reigns and challenged him to a match at Backlash. Reigns accepted, confirming what is expected to be the main event. Seth Rollins also challenged Bron Breakker to a match at the event, creating a second top Raw singles matchup. The IYO SKY vs. Asuka feud, which intensified when Asuka attacked SKY during her title match against Becky Lynch, is also building toward Backlash.

For full match-by-match analysis, see WWE Backlash 2026 preview .

WrestleMania 42 | Key Results

WrestleMania 42 took place in 2026 and served as the creative reset point for WWE's current title picture. The defining result was Roman Reigns defeating CM Punk to capture the World Heavyweight Championship, closing the WrestleMania chapter of the Punk-Reigns rivalry.

WrestleMania 42 also saw the continuation of the Bloodline saga, which has been WWE's central storyline since Summer 2020, spanning Roman Reigns' historic run as Universal and later Undisputed WWE Champion, the integration of The Usos, Sami Zayn, and Solo Sikoa, and the eventual fracture that produced multiple Bloodline factions heading into 2026.

Jacob Fatu, a real-life member of the Anoa'i wrestling family (the same family as Roman Reigns, The Usos, and Rikishi), debuted in WWE in 2024 after a long run in MLW and quickly became a top heel, aligning initially with Solo Sikoa before his own heel-to-tweener trajectory in 2026.

WWE Upcoming Events | 2026 Calendar

WWE SummerSlam 2026 tickets went on pre-sale in late April 2026 and general sale is expected shortly after. WrestleCon has also confirmed it will run alongside SummerSlam weekend, a tradition that began during WrestleMania and has expanded to other major WWE events.

Notable WWE Superstars | Active Roster 2026

WWE's current roster includes over 200 signed talent across Raw, SmackDown, and NXT. The following superstars are central to the current on-screen product heading into May 2026:

WWE also conducted a significant wave of roster cuts in late April 2026, releasing talents including Kairi Sane, Apollo Crews, Dexter Lumis, Rowan, Zelina Vega, and Alba Fyre, among others. IYO Sky commented publicly on Kairi Sane's release after they had been longtime tag partners as Damage CTRL.
